scene_title: A seaport in Cyprus. An open place near the quay

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: What from the cape can you discern at sea?

lines.speaker: FIRST GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Nothing at all: it is a highwrought flood;

lines.speaker: FIRST GENTLEMAN
lines.text: I cannot, ’twixt the heaven and the main,

lines.speaker: FIRST GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Descry a sail.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Methinks the wind hath spoke aloud at land;

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: A fuller blast ne’er shook our battlements:

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: If it hath ruffian’d so upon the sea,

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: What ribs of oak, when mountains melt on them,

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Can hold the mortise? What shall we hear of this?

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: A segregation of the Turkish fleet:

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: For do but stand upon the foaming shore,

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The chidden billow seems to pelt the clouds;

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The wind-shaked surge, with high and monstrous mane,

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Seems to cast water on the burning bear,

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: And quench the guards of the ever-fixed pole:

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: I never did like molestation view

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: On the enchafed flood.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: If that the Turkish fleet

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Be not enshelter’d and embay’d, they are drown’d:

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: It is impossible they bear it out.

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: News, lads! our wars are done.

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The desperate tempest hath so bang’d the Turks,

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: That their designment halts: a noble ship of Venice

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Hath seen a grievous wreck and sufferance

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: On most part of their fleet.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: How! is this true?

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The ship is here put in,

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: A Veronesa; Michael Cassio,

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Lieutenant to the warlike Moor Othello,

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Is come on shore: the Moor himself at sea,

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: And is in full commission here for Cyprus.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: I am glad on’t; ’tis a worthy governor.

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: But this same Cassio, though he speak of comfort

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Touching the Turkish loss, yet he looks sadly,

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: And prays the Moor be safe; for they were parted

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: With foul and violent tempest.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Pray heavens he be;

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: For I have served him, and the man commands

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Like a full soldier. Let’s to the seaside, ho!

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: As well to see the vessel that’s come in

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: As to throw out our eyes for brave Othello,

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Even till we make the main and the aerial blue

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: An indistinct regard.

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Come, let’s do so:

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: For every minute is expectancy

lines.speaker: THIRD GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Of more arrivance.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Thanks, you the valiant of this warlike isle,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That so approve the Moor! O, let the heavens

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Give him defence against the elements,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: For I have lost us him on a dangerous sea.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: Is he well shipp’d?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: His bark is stoutly timber’d, his pilot

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Of very expert and approved allowance;

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Therefore my hopes, not surfeited to death,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Stand in bold cure. A cry within “A sail, a sail, a sail!”

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: What noise?

lines.speaker: FOURTH GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The town is empty; on the brow o’ the sea

lines.speaker: FOURTH GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Stand ranks of people, and they cry “A sail!”

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: My hopes do shape him for the governor. Guns heard.

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMEN
lines.text: They do discharge their shot of courtesy:

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMEN
lines.text: Our friends at least.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: I pray you, sir, go forth,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: And give us truth who ’tis that is arrived.

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: I shall.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: But, good lieutenant, is your general wived?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Most fortunately: he hath achieved a maid

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That paragons description and wild fame;

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: One that excels the quirks of blazoning pens,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: And in the essential vesture of creation

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Does tire the ingener.

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: ’Tis one Iago, ancient to the general.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Has had most favourable and happy speed:

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Tempests themselves, high seas, and howling winds,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: The gutter’d rocks and congregated sands—

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Traitors ensteep’d to clog the guiltless keel—

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: As having sense of beauty, do omit

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Their mortal natures, letting go safely by

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: The divine Desdemona.

lines.speaker: MONTANO
lines.text: What is she?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: She that I spake of, our great captain’s captain,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Left in the conduct of the bold Iago,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Whose footing here anticipates our thoughts

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: A sennight’s speed. Great Jove, Othello guard,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: And swell his sail with thine own powerful breath,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That he may bless this bay with his tall ship,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Make love’s quick pants in Desdemona’s arms,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Give renew’d fire to our extincted spirits

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: And bring all Cyprus comfort!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I thank you, valiant Cassio.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: What tidings can you tell me of my lord?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: He is not yet arrived: nor know I aught

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: But that he’s well and will be shortly here.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: O, but I fear—How lost you company?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: The great contention of the sea and skies

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Parted our fellowship—But, hark! a sail.

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: They give their greeting to the citadel;

lines.speaker: SECOND GENTLEMAN
lines.text: This likewise is a friend.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: See for the news.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Good ancient, you are welcome. To Emilia. Welcome, mistress.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Let it not gall your patience, good Iago,

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That I extend my manners; ’tis my breeding

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: That gives me this bold show of courtesy. Kissing her.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Sir, would she give you so much of her lips

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As of her tongue she oft bestows on me,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You’ll have enough.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Alas, she has no speech.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: In faith, too much;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I find it still, when I have list to sleep:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Marry, before your ladyship, I grant,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She puts her tongue a little in her heart,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And chides with thinking.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: You have little cause to say so.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Come on, come on; you are pictures out of doors,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Bells in your parlors, wild-cats in your kitchens,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Saints in your injuries, devils being offended,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Players in your housewifery, and housewives in your beds.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: O, fie upon thee, slanderer!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Nay, it is true, or else I am a Turk:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: You rise to play and go to bed to work.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: You shall not write my praise.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: No, let me not.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: What wouldst thou write of me, if thou shouldst

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: praise me?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O gentle lady, do not put me to’t;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For I am nothing, if not critical.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Come on, assay. There’s one gone to the harbour?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Ay, madam.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: I am not merry; but I do beguile

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: The thing I am, by seeming otherwise.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Come, how wouldst thou praise me?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I am about it; but indeed my invention

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Comes from my pate as birdlime does from frize;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: It plucks out brains and all: but my Muse labours,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And thus she is deliver’d.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If she be fair and wise, fairness and wit,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The one’s for use, the other useth it.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Well praised! How if she be black and witty?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If she be black, and thereto have a wit,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She’ll find a white that shall her blackness fit.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Worse and worse.

lines.speaker: EMILIA
lines.text: How if fair and foolish?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She never yet was foolish that was fair;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For even her folly help’d her to an heir.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: These are old fond paradoxes to make fools laugh i’ the alehouse.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: What miserable praise hast thou for her that’s foul and foolish?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: There’s none so foul and foolish thereunto,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But does foul pranks which fair and wise ones do.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: O heavy ignorance! thou praisest the worst best. But what praise couldst

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: thou bestow on a deserving woman indeed, one that, in the authority

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: of her merit, did justly put on the vouch of very malice

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: itself?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She that was ever fair and never proud,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Had tongue at will and yet was never loud,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Never lack’d gold and yet went never gay,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Fled from her wish and yet said “Now I may,”

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She that being anger’d, her revenge being nigh,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Bade her wrong stay and her displeasure fly,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She that in wisdom never was so frail

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To change the cod’s head for the salmon’s tail;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She that could think and ne’er disclose her mind,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: See suitors following and not look behind,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: She was a wight, if ever such wight were—

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: To do what?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: To suckle fools and chronicle small beer.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: O most lame and impotent conclusion! Do not learn of him, Emilia,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: though he be thy husband. How say you, Cassio? is he not

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: a most profane and liberal counsellor?

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: He speaks home, madam: You may relish him more in the soldier

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: than in the scholar.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: He takes her by the palm: ay, well said, whisper: with as

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: little a web as this will I ensnare as great a fly

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: as Cassio. Ay, smile upon her, do; I will gyve thee in

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: thine own courtship. You say true; ’tis so, indeed: if such tricks

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: as these strip you out of your lieutenantry, it had been better

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: you had not kissed your three fingers so oft, which now again

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: you are most apt to play the sir in. Very good; well

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: kissed! an excellent courtesy! ’tis so, indeed. Yet again your fingers to

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: your lips? would they were clyster-pipes for your sake! The Moor! I

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: know his trumpet.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: ’Tis truly so.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Let’s meet him and receive him.

lines.speaker: CASSIO
lines.text: Lo, where he comes!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: O my fair warrior!

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: My dear Othello!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It gives me wonder great as my content

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: To see you here before me. O my soul’s joy!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: If after every tempest come such calms,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: May the winds blow till they have waken’d death!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And let the labouring bark climb hills of seas

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Olympus-high and duck again as low

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: As hell’s from heaven! If it were now to die,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: ’Twere now to be most happy; for, I fear,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: My soul hath her content so absolute

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That not another comfort like to this

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Succeeds in unknown fate.

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: The heavens forbid

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: But that our loves and comforts should increase,

lines.speaker: DESDEMONA
lines.text: Even as our days do grow!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Amen to that, sweet powers!

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I cannot speak enough of this content;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: It stops me here; it is too much of joy:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: And this, and this, the greatest discords be Kissing her.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: That e’er our hearts shall make!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: O, you are well tuned now!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But I’ll set down the pegs that make this music,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: As honest as I am.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Come, let us to the castle.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: News, friends; our wars are done, the Turks

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: are drown’d.

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: How does my old acquaintance of this isle?

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Honey, you shall be well desired in Cyprus;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I have found great love amongst them. O my sweet,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: I prattle out of fashion, and I dote

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: In mine own comforts. I prithee, good Iago,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Go to the bay and disembark my coffers:

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Bring thou the master to the citadel;

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: He is a good one, and his worthiness

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Does challenge much respect. Come, Desdemona,

lines.speaker: OTHELLO
lines.text: Once more, well met at Cyprus.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Do thou meet me presently at the harbour. Come hither. If thou

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: be’st valiant—as, they say, base men being in love have then a

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: nobility in their natures more than is native to them—list me. The

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: lieutenant tonight watches on the court of guard:—first, I must tell thee

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: this—Desdemona is directly in love with him.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: With him! why, ’tis not possible.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Lay thy finger thus, and let thy soul be instructed. Mark me

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: with what violence she first loved the Moor, but for bragging and

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: telling her fantastical lies: and will she love him still for prating?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: let not thy discreet heart think it. Her eye must be fed;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: and what delight shall she have to look on the devil? When

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: the blood is made dull with the act of sport, there should

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: be, again to inflame it and to give satiety a fresh appetite,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: loveliness in favour, sympathy in years, manners and beauties; all which the

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Moor is defective in: now, for want of these required conveniences, her

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: delicate tenderness will find itself abused, begin to heave the gorge, disrelish

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: and abhor the Moor; very nature will instruct her in it and

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: compel her to some second choice. Now, sir, this granted—as it is

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: a most pregnant and unforced position—who stands so eminent in the degree

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: of this fortune as Cassio does? a knave very voluble; no further

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: conscionable than in putting on the mere form of civil and humane

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: seeming, for the better compassing of his salt and most hidden loose

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: affection? why, none; why, none: a slipper and subtle knave, a finder

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: of occasions, that has an eye can stamp and counterfeit advantages, though

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: true advantage never present itself; a devilish knave. Besides, the knave is

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: handsome, young, and hath all those requisites in him that folly and

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: green minds look after: a pestilent complete knave; and the woman hath

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: found him already.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I cannot believe that in her; she’s full of most blessed condition.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Blessed fig’s-end! the wine she drinks is made of grapes: if she

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: had been blessed, she would never have loved the Moor. Blessed pudding!

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Didst thou not see her paddle with the palm of his hand?

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: didst not mark that?

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Yes, that I did; but that was but courtesy.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Lechery, by this hand; an index and obscure prologue to the history

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: of lust and foul thoughts. They met so near with their lips

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: that their breaths embraced together. Villainous thoughts, Roderigo! when these mutualities so

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: marshal the way, hard at hand comes the master and main exercise,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: the incorporate conclusion, Pish! But, sir, be you ruled by me: I

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: have brought you from Venice. Watch you to-night; for the command, I’ll

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: lay’t upon you. Cassio knows you not. I’ll not be far from

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: you: do you find some occasion to anger Cassio, either by speaking

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: too loud, or tainting his discipline; or from what other course you

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: please, which the time shall more favourably minister.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Well.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Sir, he is rash and very sudden in choler, and haply may

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: strike at you: provoke him, that he may; for even out of

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: that will I cause these of Cyprus to mutiny; whose qualification shall

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: come into no true taste again but by the displanting of Cassio.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: So shall you have a shorter journey to your desires by the

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: means I shall then have to prefer them; and the impediment most

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: profitably removed, without the which there were no expectation of our prosperity.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: I will do this, if I can bring it to any opportunity.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I warrant thee. Meet me by and by at the citadel: I

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: must fetch his necessaries ashore. Farewell.

lines.speaker: RODERIGO
lines.text: Adieu.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That Cassio loves her, I do well believe it;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That she loves him, ’tis apt and of great credit:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: The Moor, howbeit that I endure him not,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Is of a constant, loving, noble nature,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And I dare think he’ll prove to Desdemona

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: A most dear husband. Now, I do love her too;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Not out of absolute lust, though peradventure

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I stand accountant for as great a sin,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: But partly led to diet my revenge,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For that I do suspect the lusty Moor

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Hath leap’d into my seat; the thought whereof

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Doth, like a poisonous mineral, gnaw my inwards;

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And nothing can or shall content my soul

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Till I am even’d with him, wife for wife,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Or failing so, yet that I put the Moor

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: At least into a jealousy so strong

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: That judgment cannot cure. Which thing to do,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: If this poor trash of Venice, whom I trash

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For his quick hunting, stand the putting on,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: I’ll have our Michael Cassio on the hip,

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Abuse him to the Moor in the rank garb—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For I fear Cassio with my night-cap too—

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Make the Moor thank me, love me and reward me.

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: For making him egregiously an ass

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: And practising upon his peace and quiet

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Even to madness. ’Tis here, but yet confused:

lines.speaker: IAGO
lines.text: Knavery’s plain face is never seen till used.

stage_directions.text: How now! who has put in?
Enter Desdemona, Emilia, Iago, Roderigo, and Attendants.
O, behold, The riches of the ship is come on shore! Ye men of Cyprus, let her have your knees. Hail to thee, lady! and the grace of heaven, Before, behind thee, and on every hand, Enwheel thee round!
Within “A sail, a sail!” Guns heard.

glosses.definition: scolded wave; a towering wave that seems to strike the sky
monstrous crest of the wave — likened to a horse's mane
the Pole Star; the still axis of the heavens
a ship from Verona, hired by Venice
a soldier of complete skill and character
the witty turns of poetic praise
the very substance of her nature
scolds when she dares to think
playing the saint while injuring others
keep tedious household accounts; concern herself with trivia
fetter; shackle — Iago plans to use Cassio's courtesies as chains
gentleman-courtier; one who plays the gallant
enema tubes — a coarse, dismissive image for Cassio's kissed fingers
Othello's term of endearment for Desdemona, honoring her spirit that shared his campaigning
loosen the strings of this harmony — a musician's figure for Iago's plan to create discord
vigorous Moor — Iago's insinuation is lewd

annotations.title: The storm offstage and the plot's deliverance
annotations.body:
Between 1.3 and 2.1 the Turkish fleet — the political engine that brought Othello to Cyprus — is destroyed by a storm the audience never sees. Shakespeare has dispensed with his play's ostensible action. The military plot is over before the personal one has begun. What follows will not be a war play.

This is the sharpest structural decision in the tragedy. Cyprus, a forward outpost of empire, becomes a domestic stage: the garrison town where soldiers drink, women wait, and rumor circulates. The stripping-away of the external enemy concentrates the play's violence inward, where it has no legitimate outlet. The military discipline Othello embodies in Act 1 has nowhere to act. Iago supplies the war he no longer has.
sources: G. R. Hibbard, 'Othello and the Pattern of Shakespearean Tragedy', *Shakespeare Survey* 21 (1968)

annotations.title: Iago's banter as method: the reading of Cassio's manners
annotations.body:
Iago's aside during Cassio and Desdemona's courteous greeting — 'He takes her by the palm: ay, well said, whisper. With as little a web as this will I ensnare as great a fly as Cassio' — shows his core procedure. He is not inventing evidence; he is reading ordinary behavior as if it were guilty. A hand kiss, a bow, a polite kindness are reinterpreted as tokens of conspiracy. The technique requires no lies at all in the early stages; it requires only the audience — the one on stage, Othello — to watch through Iago's frame.

This is why the play resists easy moral reduction. Evidence and interpretation are indistinguishable for the jealous. Iago's genius is to occupy the interpretive position ahead of Othello, so that when Othello arrives, the reading is already in place.
sources: Stanley Cavell, 'Othello and the Stake of the Other', in *Disowning Knowledge* (2003)
Paul A. Kottman, *Tragic Conditions in Shakespeare* (2009)

trials.prompt: What has happened to the Turkish fleet between the previous scene and this one?
options: It has landed on Cyprus and begun a siege
A storm has destroyed it before it could reach Cyprus
It has turned back to Istanbul at the Sultan's orders
It has been defeated in naval battle by Othello

trials.prompt: Why does Iago speak aside during Cassio's courteous greetings to Desdemona — 'With as little a web as this will I ensnare as great a fly as Cassio'?
options: He is reminding himself to warn Cassio of genuine dangers
He is planning to reinterpret ordinary politeness as evidence of adultery
He is worried Desdemona will discover his scheme
He is composing a letter he will send later

trials.prompt: Iago's wordplay with Desdemona on women — deserving, undeserving, wise, foolish, fair, black — functions primarily as:
options: Genuine literary criticism
A way of entertaining her while they wait for Othello's ship
A demonstration of his capacity to reduce every woman to misogynist couplets, practicing the voice he will later use with Othello
Friendly banter typical of Elizabethan courtship

trials.prompt: What does Iago falsely suggest Cassio and Desdemona have been doing earlier?
options: Planning a military campaign together
Flirting in ways that betray mutual lust
Writing a letter to Iago
Arranging a musical performance

trials.prompt: Othello greets Desdemona with 'O my fair warrior!' Why 'warrior'?
options: She has trained in combat aboard the ship
He honors her spirit as the partner who shared his campaigning life
Venice grants military rank to married women
The word is sarcastic, mocking her delicate appearance
